coredata.dc:title: Zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate functionalized carbon quantum dots as water-based lubricant additive for friction and wear reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Shenghua
Cui, Yuhong
Chen, Xin
Shi, Junqin
Liu, Shujuan
Ye, Qian
Zhou, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have successfully prepared carbon quantum dots with abundant edge-OH (CQDs-OH) via hydrothermal reaction, and then the zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate (ZDS) was self-assembled onto CQDs surface via chemical bond between the catechol group of ZDS and the edge-OH of CQDs. Due to the good dispersion stability of quantum dots endowed by ZDS, CQD-ZDS exhibits excellent lubricating effect as a water-based lubricating additive. Meanwhile, the tribochemical reaction induced by CQD-ZDS can form a stable carbon and metal oxide protective film on the wear surface. Compared with pure water, the average friction coefficient (COF) decreases from 0.5 to 0.11. The hydration process of ZDS modified CQDs is simulated by molecular dynamics simulation, and its lubricating mechanism is explored.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate
Self-assembly
Water-based lubricant additive

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic presentation of the preparation process for CQDs-ZDS. (b) TEM image, (c, d) HRTEM images (insets: FFT patterns), (e) Particle size distribution of CQDs-ZDS, (f) AFM image of CQDs-ZDS and height profile diagram and (g) photographs of CQDs-ZDS under ultraviolet light (365 nm).

(a) FT-IR spectra of CQDs-OH and CQDs-ZDS. (b) Zeta potential in H2O of the typical samples. High-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs-ZDS: (c) N 1s and (d) S 2p. (e) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-OH and CQDs-ZDS, (f) Raman spectra of CQDs-ZDS.

(a) COF curves of the as-prepared samples, (b) COF curves of CQDs-ZDS at different addition of 0.1–3.0 wt%. COF versus time curves of CQDs-ZDS under (c) variable load and (d) frequency. (e) Average COF curves of CQDs-ZDS under different conditions, (f) wear volumes of H2O, 1.0 wt% CQDs-OH + water and 1.0 wt% CQDs-ZDS + water, and (g) the 3D contour images.
(a) Raman spectroscopy of wear mark, (b) signal strength image and (c) optical image of wear mark. High-resolution XPS spectra of wear mark; (d) Fe 2p3 (e) O 1s and (f) S 2p.

Snapshots of equilibrium configurations of (a) CQDs-OH and (b) CQDs-ZDS in an aqueous environment for 10 ns. Color codes: blue and cyan represent free water molecule, gray represent small molecules grafted on CQDs, purple represents CQDs, red, yellow and green represent bound water molecules.
Hydrodynamic size distribution of CQDs-OH and CQDs-ZDS.
Lubrication mechanism of CQDs-ZDS as water‑based lubricant additive.

head.ce:title: Zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate functionalized carbon quantum dots as water-based lubricant additive for friction and wear reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing
Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have successfully prepared carbon quantum dots with abundant edge-OH (CQDs-OH) via hydrothermal reaction, and then the zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate (ZDS) was self-assembled onto CQDs surface via chemical bond between the catechol group of ZDS and the edge-OH of CQDs. Due to the good dispersion stability of quantum dots endowed by ZDS, CQD-ZDS exhibits excellent lubricating effect as a water-based lubricating additive. Meanwhile, the tribochemical reaction induced by CQD-ZDS can form a stable carbon and metal oxide protective film on the wear surface. Compared with pure water, the average friction coefficient (COF) decreases from 0.5 to 0.11. The hydration process of ZDS modified CQDs is simulated by molecular dynamics simulation, and its lubricating mechanism is explored.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear usually lead to material and energy consumption, causes great economic losses. Lubrication is of great importance for reducing energy dissipation and improving the durability of mechanical equipment [1]. The adoption of appropriate lubricants is one of the most convenient and low-cost ways to solve the issues. Compared with oil-based lubricants, water-based lubricants have attracted great attention due to low-cost, excellent cooling performance, flame retardancy, and environmental friendliness [2]. However, pure water cannot act as an effective lubricant due to its low viscosity, easy-corrosive and poor load-bearing capacity [3]. Therefore, the adoption of appropriate additives is a widely used and convenient approach to improve the lubricating performance of water lubricants and broaden its application prospects [4].

Nanomaterials, such as carbon materials, MoS2, and WS2, have caught increasing attention and become the research hotspots owing to unique physicochemical properties. Because of its small size and good lubricity, it has a broad application prospect as a solid lubricant or lubricant additive, and can improve the tribological performance of equipment [5]. Carbon nanomaterials, such as fullerenes [6], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [7], carbon nanosheets [8], carbon nanospheres (CNS) [9], onion carbon [10] and their derivatives were studied widely and showed excellent anti-wear, reducing-friction performance as lubricant additives due to their small size, high corrosion resistance, and morphological and thermal stability and their self-lubricating effects [11]. However, the dispersibility of these carbon nanomaterials in lubricants is poor due to their chemical inertness, thereby restricting their practical application. It is important to improve their dispersible stability in water, which have affected the tribological performance of lubricant [5]. By surface modification or introducing hydrophilic groups in carbon nanomaterials, the dispersion stability and lubrication effect of carbon nanomaterials in water can be improved [12]. But it is still difficult to maintain the long-term dispersibility and storage stability of carbon nanomaterials in the dispersion medium after surface modification, which is also vital factor that limiting their widespread application in industries [13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs, <10 nm), a novel type of zero-dimension carbon nanomaterials material, have been used in various fields of photovoltaic devices and biomedicine due to their outstanding photoluminescent, robust chemical inertness, good biocompatibility and high aqueous solubility [14]. Compared to conventional carbon materials, CQDs exhibits natural dispersion stability in polar solvent [15], which is due to the high specific surface area, small particle size, and rich oxygen-containing functional groups [16]. The CQDs with multilayer structure can be regarded as a fragment of graphite [17], so, they can be used as lubricant additives due to low interlayer shear strength, nano-bearing effect, etc. Qiang et al. have perpared water-soluble graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by hydrothermal method, the as-prepared GQDs as aqueous nanoadditive can reduce COF and wear rate by 42.5% and 58.5% respectively [18]. Hu et al. have prepared water-soluble carbon dots by one-pot pyrolysis of ammonium citrate, the as-prepared CDs as water based nanoadditives exhibit low COF, good wear-resistance and effective corrosion inhibitor effect [19]. Liu et al. have prepared a novel graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) as additive to achieve super-lubrication in ethylene glycol aqueous solution, the COF was reduced to 0.0068 and the wear volume was decreased by 90.0% [20]. Furthermore, the functionalized CQD have also been developed for lubrication applications via surface modification. Qu et al. reported an efficient method to suppress the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs via surface modification, which was to construct functionalized CD by chemically bonded branched-chain polythylenimine (b-PEI) on the surface [21]. Wang et al. prepared a series of functionalized CQDs with different anionic groups, which displayed excellent dispersibility and long-term stability in PEG-200, the oleate groups modified CQDs exhibited the best lubrication performance, good load-bearing capacity and operational stability [22]. Herein, we report a simple and convenient method for preparation of functionalized graphene quantum dots via a hydrothermal reaction and then surface modification with zwitterionic molecule. The zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate (ZDS) can self-assemble onto the CQDs surface via chemical bond between the catechol group of ZDS and the edge-OH of CQDs, which endow quantum dots with good dispersion and stability in water due to good hydrophilic performance. The tribological properties of CQD-ZDS as a water-based lubricant additive were investigated on a universal testing machine (UMT) in a ball-disk linear reciprocating model. The hydration mechanism of the as-prepared CQD-ZDS was calculated via molecular dynamics simulation, and their lubrication mechanism was provided and discussed in detail.

Pyrene (95%) was purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide, nitric acid (AR 99%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dopamine hydrochloride, 1,3-propanesulfonolactone (98%) and iodomethane were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from Barnstead/Thermolyne's NANO pure Infinity system.

1,3,6-trinitropyrene was prepared by simple water reaction with pyrene as raw material [23]. Typically, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (5 mg/mL) was dissolved in 60 mL (0.2 M) NaOH solution and sonicated for 2 h, which was transferred into steel reactor and heated to 200 °C for 10 h. Then the product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm. Finally, the as-prepared black CQDs-OH crude solution was collected by a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane.

Dopamine hydrochloride was firstly dissolved in 100 mL EtOH, then the 416 µL of ammonia and 550 µL of 1,3-propane sultone were added into the above mixture slowly. The mixture were heated to 50 °C and stirred for 12 h to form a white precipitate, then dried under vacuum overnight to obtain dopamine sulfonate (DS). Anhydrous Na2CO3 (0.25 g, 2.4 mmol) and DS (0.33 g, 1 mmol) were added into 100 mL DMF, followed by addition of iodomethane (1.6 mL, 25 mmol) at 50 °C under N2 atmosphere. The mixture was stirred for 10 h and evaporated under reduced pressure to obtain a crude product. Afterward, 50 mL DMF/Acetone (1:10 v/v) were added into the crude product reflux at 55 °C for 2 h to obtain the target product ZDS [24].

First, water-soluble ZDS (2 mg/mL) was dissolved in 50 mL of CQDs-OH aqueous solution. Afterwards, the above mixture was placed in a 150 mL three-necked flask, and stirred in the dark at room temperature for 48 h under N2 atmosphere. Finally, the as-prepared CQDs-ZDS solution was dialyzed against a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 for two days to remove residual small molecules. The CQDs-ZDS powder was obtained by freeze-drying before further analysis.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to observe the microscopic morphology of the CQDs, and the lattice fringes were observed under HRTEM. AFM images were taken using an atomic force microscope (MFP 3D Origin+) to observe the height of the CQDs. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using a field emission scanning electron microscope (ZEISS Sigma 300). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 VersaProbe III) was used to analyze the surface chemical composition of the as-prepared samples. Raman spectra were obtained via laser confocal Raman micro spectroscopy under 532 nm. The tribological properties were tested by UMT Tribo (Bruker, USA) in a reciprocating ball-disk mode at room temperature. Si3N4 ball (φ 6.35 mm) was selected as the upper friction pair, and 304 stainless steel disc (φ 24 mm) was selected as the lower friction pair (Table S1). All tribological tests were performed with an amplitude of 1 mm. The morphology of wear scars are obtained using a three-dimensional surface profilometer (Bruker, NPFLEX). The surface potential and hydration particle size were obtained using a Zetasizer Nano zeta potential analyzer (water as solvent; 25 ◦C).

Fig. 1 a illustrated the preparation process of CQDs-ZDS. The ZDS with catecholic group can graft onto the edge of CQDs-OH through catecholic molecule self-assembly (Fig. S1) [25]. The morphology and size of CQDs-ZDS were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1a and 1b). The as-prepared CQDs-ZDS had good dispersion and uniform size, and the lateral size was concentrated in 2–3 nm. From the HR-TEM image of CQDs-ZDS, lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) plane of graphene (Fig. 1d) [23]. As shown in Fig. 1f, the height of CQDs-ZDS was about 1.5 ∼ 2 nm, indicating a quasi-spherical structure. The as-prepared CQDs-ZDS emitted aquamarine fluorescence when irradiated with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1g).

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the unmodified CQDs-OH had an obvious absorption peak at 3450 cm−1, which was attributed to the abundant –OH. The absorption peaks at 600 cm−1, 1662 cm−1, and 3350 cm−1 represented C-H, C-O, and C=C, respectively.[23] For CQDs-ZDS, the S=O stretching peak of sulfonic acid groups appeared at 1046 cm−1 demonstrating the successful modification with zwitterionic molecules ZDS [26]. The results of Zeta potential test further proved the successful self-assembly of ZDS molecule. Compared with CQDs-OH, the surface potential was increased to –32.4 eV (from −41.5 eV) after modification with ZDS, which was due to the introduction of the electrically neutral amphiphilic molecules on the surface of carbon quantum dots (Fig. 2b) [26]. The surface element composition and chemical state of the CQDs-ZDS were analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2c and 2d, the N 1s spectrum of the CQDs-ZDS displays a peak at a binding energy of about 402 eV, which is owed to the quaternary ammonium cation [C-N+(CH3)3] of ZDS molecule. The S 2p spectrum of CQDs-ZDS consisted of two peaks at 163.5 eV and 168.1 eV, corresponding to the S-C and SO3 signals respectively,[27] which also confirm the attachment of the ZDS molecule onto the CQDs. The crystal phase compositions of CQDs-ZDS and CQDs-OH were analyzed by XRD (Fig. S2), the CQDs with/without ZDS modification had obvious diffraction peaks at 26°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite structure. For the optical properties of CQDs-ZDS, the emission peak of CQDs-ZDS underwent blue shift by laser excitation at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 2e), which may be due to the change of the CQDs electronic cloud structure after introduction of zwitterionic molecule ZDS [28]. As shown in Fig. S3, the CQDs-ZDS had an obvious UV absorption peak at 230 nm [28], which was corresponded to the π-π* transition of carbon quantum dot Sp2. As shown in Fig. 2f, there were obvious G band and D band at 1580 cm−1 and 1364 cm−1 in the as-prepared CQDs-ZDS. The high broadening of the D band was caused by the defects and disordered structure of CQDs-ZDS, which can be fitted three sub-peaks. The D* band at 1220 cm−1 and the D** band at 1500 cm−1 may be due to the Sp3 phase in amorphous carbon [29]. The D band at 1364 cm−1 was considered to be the disordered vibration peak of CQDs, reflecting the structural defects at the edge of CQDs-ZDS. The G peak at 1581 cm−1 was due to the intralayer vibration of the Sp2 carbon atom [30]. ID/IG was used to characterize the graphitization degree and defect density of carbon quantum dots [9]. The result of IG/ID = 1.2 proved that the graphitization degree of the as-prepared carbon quantum dots was comparatively high [23]. From Fig. S4, it can be found that the position and intensity ratio of D band and G band of CQD with and without ZDS modification did not change significantly, and still maintained a good graphitized structure.

The tribological properties of CQDs-OH and CQDs-ZDS based lubricants were tested at the load of 15 N and the frequency of 5 Hz. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the COF of CQDs-OH based lubricants was stabilized at 0.126 after 2 min, and the CQDs-ZDS was stabilized at 0.11. Compared with pure water, the COF of CQDs-ZDS was reduced by 78%, and the friction curve was relatively smooth, which was attributed to the formation of hydration layer of zwitterionic molecules ZDS [26]. The good hydrophilicity and dispersibility of CQDs-ZDS are the main reasons for improving lubricating properties, which can make CQDs-ZDS remain in water for several months without sedimentation (Fig. S5). The lubricating performance of CQDs-ZDS based lubricant did not deteriorate with the increase of the addition amount of CQDs-ZDS. When the addition amount was 1.0 wt%, the lubricating effect reached the best (Fig. 3b and e). The anti-wear of the as-prepared additives was investigated by the wear scar of lower disks by 3D surface profilometer (Fig. 3g). The wear volume of the steel disk lubrication with water was as high as 6.0 × 105 µm3, while the value was reduced to 3.7 × 105 μm3 with the addition of 1.0 wt% CQDs-OH. The wear volume of 1.0 wt% CQDs-ZDS based lubricants can be significantly decreased to 2.9 × 105 μm3 (52% reduction). The maximum width and depth of the wear scar was also reduced to 249.1 µm from 441.5 µm, and 2.1 µm from 5.1 µm, respectively. In order to analyse the anti-wear behavior of the as-prepared CQDs-ZDS in detailed, the SEM images of the wear scar surface are provided (Fig. S6). The pure water based wear surface not only had obvious furrowing and adhesive wear, but also accompanied a large number of surface damage and secondary cutting spalling. After adding CQDs-ZDS, the valleys and ravines have got shallow and narrow, and the scratches of which was much smoother and narrower. As shown in Fig. 3c and 3d, the frequency and load can generate some effects for tribological performance of the as-prepared CQDs-ZDS. With the increase of load and frequency, the mean COF exhibited an evident downward trend, which was attributed to the polishing effect induced by robust CQDs. In detail, the tribo-pairs are macroscopically smooth, while uneven in microscopic. Higher load and quicker frequency will accelerate the diffusion of CQDs-ZDS in the contacting area and polishing of the surface, thus diminishing the friction and wear caused by the violent collision between the asperities. Moreover, the CQDs-ZDS emerged negligible failure in the wide range of load from 5 N to 15 N, as well as wide range of frequency from 1 Hz to 7 Hz, which is indicative of remarkable bearing capacity under hard working conditions.

In order to explore the lubrication mechanism of CQDs-ZDS, the lower disc of the friction pair were analyzed by XPS and Raman spectroscopy. Twenty points were selected equidistant at a length of 600 μm on the wear surface. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the D band (1364 cm−1) and G band (1580 cm−1) similar to the prepared CQDs were observed in the range of 1000–1600 cm−1, demonstrating the formation of a carbon film on the friction surface. The signals of peak D and peak G represented by the carbon film were detected in the range of 0–400 μm, and the iron oxides (200–750 cm−1) [31] were exposed in the regions not covered by the carbon film at 450 μm and 550 μm (Fig. 4b). The 2D band and D + D' band proved that the formation of carbon film was a coexistence state of order and disorder [18]. Futhermore, the composition of the friction film was analyzed by XPS. Exhibited in the Fig. 4d, 4e and 4f, the peaks of Fe 2p3 at 709–711.8 eV in Fig. 4d can be attributed to FeO(OH) and iron oxides (FeO, Fe3O4, Fe2O3, FeO(OH)), which meets with the O1s peaks at 531.3 eV and 530.1 eV [32]. The peaks at 713.9 eV can be attributed to ferric sulfate, which corresponds to the peak of S 2p at 168.3 eV [12]. Trace amounts of N signals are observed on the scar (Fig. S7b), which indicates the formation of carbonitride. In brief, the above results indicate that a complex tribo-chemical reactions occurred during the friction process [33], and form a boundary protection film composed of carbonitride, FeO(OH), iron oxides and sulfides. The introduction of N and S elements made CQDs-ZDS exhibit excellent antifriction and antiwear effects.

Fig. 5 a and b showed the density distribution of water molecules around CQDs-OH and CQDs-ZDS, and the color from red to blue represented a gradual decrease in the density of water molecules. We considered the red, yellow and green parts of the water molecules as carbon quantum dot bound water molecules, and the blue and cyan parts as free water molecules. R1 and R2 indicated the radii of water spheres formed by CQDs-OH and CQDs-ZDS, respectively. It was obvious that R2 > R1, which indicated that the grafted ZDS improved the hydration ability of carbon quantum dots, and the mutual exclusion effect between hydration layers improves the dispersion stability of quantum dots [34]. During the friction process, the quantum dots can maintain a small size for a long time, and enter the friction surface easily to form a carbon film, thus avoided the friction pair direct contact, achieved good anti-friction and anti-wear performance.

Scheme 1 demonstrated the lubrication mechanism of CQDs-ZDS in the friction process. During the friction process, the contact point of the friction pair releases low-energy electrons during the friction process, so the surface of the metal friction pair has positively charged. The surface potential of quantum dots was negative, and it was easy to be adsorbed on the surface of the friction pair through electrostatic adsorption during the friction process, forming a stable adsorption film [35]. In boundary lubrication, the carbon film avoided the friction pair direct contact, and display a good anti-friction and anti-wear performance. In addition, CQDs-ZDS can be deposited or adsorbed in cracks, scratches and holes on the friction surface to dynamically repair micro-damage on the friction surface. It can not only form a protective film on the friction surface [36], but also has a repair effect by filling deep scratches, which is the direct cause of remarkable friction reduction and anti-wear performance under high load and frequency [37]. Meanwhile, nanoscale spherical CQDs-ZDS can enter the contact area of the friction surface and play the role of ball bearings, changing sliding friction into rolling friction, thus significantly reducing friction and wear in the contact area [38,39].

Good dispersion stability of quantum dots is also an important reason for the improvement of lubrication effect. Because the surface of the particles contains abundant oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and phenolic hydroxyl, which have the strong polarity, the hydrogen bonds can be formed with water molecules by dipole force, thus forming a hydration layer around the particles. In the process of friction, when the particles approach each other, the hydration layer will be squeezed and deformed, and it will try to restore the original directional arrangement of water molecules in the hydration film. This makes the hydration layer exhibit elasticity, which becomes the resistance when the particles approach each other. The structure of hydration layer is described by DLS and simulation. It was proved that the thickness of hydration layer before modification was only about 1–2 molecular layers, while ZDS hugged more water molecules like an extended arm, so it was relatively strong activity and showed strong hydrophilicity. After modification with ZDS, the hydration layer expands outward, and the hydration particle size is increased to about 4.6 nm to achieve the effect of microscopic dispersion stability (Fig. 6 ). The good dispersion of carbon quantum dots in water can enter the contact area of the friction pair during in the friction process, which is the premise of forming a continuous protective film.

In this paper, we have reported a simple and convenient method for preparation of CQDs with abundant edge-OH via hydrothermal reaction, and then the zwitterionic ZDS was self-assembled to the surface of CQDs via chemical bond between the catechol group of ZDS and the edge-OH of CQDs. The as-prepared CQDs-ZDS have long-term dispersibility and storage stability in water, which can remain in water for several months without sedimentation. The as-prepared CQD-ZDS exhibits excellent lubrication effect as a water-based lubricating additive under different concentrations, frequencies, and loading conditions. Compared with pure water, the average COF of 1 wt% CQDs-ZDS decreases from 0.5 to 0.11 and the wear volume decreased by 44%, and the bearing capacity can reach from 5 N to 15 N. The improvement of the lubricating properties of CQDs-ZDS was mainly attributed to the hydration of amphiphilic molecules in a water-rich environment, which accelerated the filling and repair of CQDs on the worn surface to form a stable carbon film, and at the same time formed a chemical protective film through tribochemical reaction.

Shenghua Xue: Writing – original draft preparation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuhong Cui: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xin Chen: Data curation. Junqin Shi: Molecular dynamics simulation. Shujuan Liu: Review & editing. Qian Ye: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Feng Zhou: Supervision, Review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A2046, 51972272), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.156411.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Compatibility between tribological surfaces and lubricant additives—How friction and wear reduction can be controlled by surface/lube synergies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Neville A, Morina A, Haque T, Voong M. Compatibility between tribological surfaces and lubricant additives—How friction and wear reduction can be controlled by surface/lube synergies. Tribol Int. 2007;40:1680-95.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lubricating a bright future: Lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cai M, Guo R, Zhou F, Liu W. Lubricating a bright future: Lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission. Science China Technological Sciences. 2013;56:2888-913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized two-dimensional covalent organic frameworks as additives for aqueous lubricants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang T, Lu Q, Zhang X, Liu S, Ye Q, Zhou F. Fabrication of polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized two-dimensional covalent organic frameworks as additives for aqueous lubricants. Tribol Int. 2022;174:107737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced developments in environmentally friendly lubricants for water-based drilling fluid: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao X, Li D, Zhu H, Ma J, An Y. Advanced developments in environmentally friendly lubricants for water-based drilling fluid: a review. RSC Advances. 2022;12:22853-68.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zwitterionic microgel-functionalized gallium-based liquid-metal nanodroplets as aqueous lubricant additives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: He B, Wang P, Lu Q, Du Y, Liu S, Ye Q, et al. Zwitterionic microgel-functionalized gallium-based liquid-metal nanodroplets as aqueous lubricant additives. Tribol Int. 2023;177:107952.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of lubrication properties of nano-oil by controlling the amount of fullerene nanoparticle additives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee J, Cho S, Hwang Y, Lee C, Kim SH. Enhancement of Lubrication Properties of Nano-oil by Controlling the Amount of Fullerene Nanoparticle Additives. Tribology Letters. 2007;28:203-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urea modified fluorinated carbon nanotubes: unique self-dispersed characteristic in water and high tribological performance as water-based lubricant additives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Min C, He Z, Liu D, Zhang K, Dong C. Urea modified fluorinated carbon nanotubes: unique self-dispersed characteristic in water and high tribological performance as water-based lubricant additives. New Journal of Chemistry. 2019;43:14684-93.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of porous g-C3N4 nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang C, Zhang G, Zhang H, Li Z, Wen Y. One-pot synthesis of porous g-C3N4 nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light. Diam Relat Mater. 2021;116:108416.
